definition: An object that has a lasting magnetic field without needing a power supply. Common permanent magnets include bar magnets and horseshoe magnets. They attract iron and other magnetic materials.

Every magnet has two poles – a north pole and a south pole. Like poles repel each other; unlike poles attract each other. The poles cannot be separated – if a magnet is broken in two, each piece has its own north and south pole.
The region around a magnet where the magnetic force acts. The field is invisible but can be visualised with iron filings or field lines. The field is strongest close to the poles.

Lines that show the direction and strength of a magnetic field. They run from the north pole to the south pole outside the magnet. Closely spaced lines indicate a strong field; widely spaced lines indicate a weak field.

A navigation tool with a movable needle made of magnetised steel. The needle's north pole points towards Earth's magnetic north pole. Earth's interior acts like a giant magnet and creates a magnetic field around the whole planet.

The relationship between electricity and magnetism. An electric current always creates a magnetic field around it. Conversely, a changing magnetic field can create an electric current. Electromagnetism is the basis for electric motors, generators and transformers.

A conductor wound into a number of turns. When current flows through the coil, the magnetic field is amplified – each turn contributes to a combined, powerful field. Coils are the basis of electromagnets, motors and generators.

A magnet created by a current-carrying coil, often wound around an iron core. Unlike permanent magnets, an electromagnet can be switched on and off. Its strength is controlled by the current and the number of turns.

A machine that converts electrical energy into kinetic (mechanical) energy. A current-carrying coil in a magnetic field experiences a force that causes it to rotate. Electric motors are found in everything from electric cars to household appliances.

The phenomenon whereby a changing magnetic field creates an electric voltage in a conductor. If a magnet moves into a coil, a current is produced. Induction is the underlying principle of generators and transformers.

A machine that converts kinetic energy into electrical energy – the reverse of an electric motor. A coil is rotated in a magnetic field, producing induction and generating alternating current. Power stations use generators driven by water, wind or steam.

Electric current whose direction alternates back and forth at regular intervals. In Sweden, the mains supply alternates at 50 Hz (50 times per second). Generators naturally produce alternating current, unlike batteries which produce direct current.

A device that changes the voltage level of alternating current using induction. It consists of two coils wound around a shared iron core. A transformer can step up or step down the voltage, enabling efficient transmission of electricity in the power grid.

The rate at which electrical energy is converted, measured in watts (W). Calculated as power = voltage × current (P = U × I). A 100 W light bulb consumes twice as much energy per second as a 50 W bulb.

namn_native: Power stations and the electricity grid
definition: Power stations produce electricity that is then transported via the grid to homes and industry. The voltage is raised to hundreds of thousands of volts to minimise energy losses over long distances. Local transformer stations then step the voltage down to the 230 V used in homes.
